data.title: Global Financial Markets Outlook: Structural Changes and Potential Volatility Analysis in 2026
data.description:
As 2026 begins, the global financial markets present a complex and dynamic landscape. According to the latest data, after achieving a return of approximately 17.9% in 2025, the S&P 500 index continues its strong momentum, approaching the 7000-point mark as of January 27, 2026, representing a year-on-year increase of approximately 21%. However, this seemingly robust growth masks internal divergences: accelerated sector rotation, volatile commodity prices, uncertainty surrounding the US dollar's trajectory, and potential expansionary volatility indicated by technical indicators. These factors collectively point to the market potentially entering a more dramatic phase. This article analyzes these key areas based on historical patterns, the latest economic data, and market indicators, exploring potential price paths and risk factors. Sector Rotation: A Sign of Market Tops

Sector rotation is an important indicator for assessing market cycles. Data from January 2026 shows that the materials and energy sectors led the S&P 500, with year-to-date gains exceeding 10% and 8% respectively, while the consumer discretionary sector lagged behind, rising only about 2%. This rotation pattern typically appears near market tops as funds shift from growth sectors to defensive and cyclical sectors. According to Goldman Sachs research, the S&P 500 is projected to have a total return of 12% in 2026, but this growth depends on broader sector participation, rather than the previous technology-led dominance.

The relative strength of the energy sector is particularly noteworthy.

The energy sector has begun to outperform the S&P 500, resembling the pattern seen before the 2022 bear market. Latest data shows that the energy index outperformed the S&P 500 by approximately 3 percentage points in January 2026. This rotation is not an isolated phenomenon but is related to the recovery in global energy demand. The International Monetary Fund (IMF), in its January 2026 World Economic Outlook update, indicated that global economic growth is projected at 3.3%, but trade policy uncertainty could exacerbate sectoral divergence. Commentary and Analysis: This rotation reflects investors' defensive stance against economic uncertainty. If energy continues to lead the market, it may indicate short-term pressure on the S&P 500, but in the medium to long term, it could drive more balanced market growth. Investors should pay attention to the correlation between energy and the index; a correlation coefficient exceeding 0.8 could trigger a broader correction. Commodity Market Dynamics: Correlation Between Copper, Oil, and Yields

Commodity markets are a key window into understanding economic cycles. The price relationship between copper and oil shows its leading indicator role. In January 2026, copper prices surged to a record high of approximately $13,000 per tonne, driven by AI demand and supply chain tensions, while oil prices remained relatively stable around $80 per barrel. This divergence is consistent with historical patterns: copper tends to lead oil price turning points. According to the latest data, copper prices rose by about 50% in 2025, while oil prices rose only slightly by 5%, reflecting strong industrial demand but ample energy supply.

S&P 500 Historical Performance: Potential Correction After Consecutive High Returns

The S&P 500 achieved returns of over 20% consecutively from 2023 to 2025, similar to the period leading up to the dot-com bubble of 1995-1998. The return in 2025 is projected at 17.9%, and 12% is expected in 2026. Historical data shows that such consecutive high returns are often followed by corrections: the market fell by about 10% after 1954-1955, and even more sharply after 1935-1936.

S&P 500 Historical Performance: Potential Correction After Consecutive High Returns

The S&P 500 achieved returns of over 20% consecutively from 2023 to 2025, similar to the period leading up to the dot-com bubble of 1995-1998. The return in 2025 is 17.9%, and it is projected to be 12% in 2026.

Dollar Trend: Channel Bottom and Potential Rebound

The US Dollar Index (DXY) is currently around 97.2, down about 10% from its 2025 high. It is approaching the lower rail of the upward channel since 2008, and seasonal data suggests a possible bottom in February. Forecasts indicate that the DXY may fall to the 92-98 range in 2026, averaging around 95 annually.

Historical data shows that dollar rebounds often depress the S&P 500, such as the rebound in early 2021 that triggered the 2022 bear market. A Trump policy analogy suggests the dollar may rebound in February.

Commentary and Analysis: A weaker dollar supports commodity price increases, but a rapid rebound could trigger a sell-off in risk assets. If the DXY breaks above 98, it may test the 100 level, exacerbating market pressure. The Fed's January 27-28 meeting is likely to maintain interest rates at 3.5%-3.75%, but data-dependent decisions increase uncertainty. Semiconductor and Technology Analysis: Divergence Signals Strengthening The semiconductor sector is projected to rise by approximately 30% in 2025, but January 2026 shows mixed performance: NVIDIA rose slightly by 0.14%, while Micron fell by 2.71%. RSI and MACD show divergence, similar to the 20% correction before November 2025. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Bollinger Bands have contracted to a 6-year low, suggesting an impending expansion. Historical data shows that contraction is often followed by increased volatility. Commentary and Analysis: Semiconductors account for approximately 10% of the S&P 500's weighting; divergence could drag down the index. Continued leadership from Broadcom and NVIDIA could support the market, but the overall sector faces a high risk of correction. Investors should pay attention to the 20% annualized correction pattern. Silver Price Volatility: Epic Expansion and Ratio Warnings

Silver prices surged to approximately $110/oz in January 2026, an increase of about 150% from 2025. Volatility exceeded 100%, and trading volume hit a record high (nearly 400 million shares in the SLV ETF). The silver/gold ratio reached extreme highs, similar to the panic periods of 1987 and 2020.

Technical analysis shows that silver prices have deviated 104% from the 200-day moving average, suggesting a bubble risk. However, high volatility opens up even greater room for swing trading.

data.title: Fidelity to launch stablecoin FIDD; Federal Reserve keeps interest rates unchanged.
data.description:
Headline

▌Fidelity to Launch Stablecoin FIDD on Ethereum

According to market news: Fidelity will launch a stablecoin, FIDD, on Ethereum that conforms to the GENIUS standard.

▌Federal Reserve Keeps Interest Rates Unchanged

The Federal Reserve announced that it will maintain the target range for the federal funds rate between 3.5% and 3.75%, in line with market expectations. Prior to this, the Federal Reserve had cut interest rates by 25 basis points in three consecutive meetings (last September, October, and December).

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $89,132.91, a 24-hour change of -0.2%; ETH price is $3,007.01, a 24-hour change of -0.5%; BNB price is $901.08, a 24-hour change of +0.3%. SOL price is $125.19, a 24-hour change of -1.6%; DOGE price is $0.125, a 24-hour change of -0.9%; XRP price is $1.91, a 24-hour change of -0.4%; TRX price is $0.2948, a 24-hour change of +0.2%. WLFI price is $0.1643, a 24-hour change of -0.2%; HYPE price is $33.78, a 24-hour change of +10.2%. Policy: The White House plans to convene a meeting next week, chaired by the White House Crypto Council, inviting senior executives from banks and the crypto industry to discuss stablecoin legislation. Friction between banks and crypto companies over the handling of stablecoin rewards has intensified in recent months. Banking groups oppose the open provisions on incentive mechanisms in the GENIUS bill, fearing it will divert deposits; crypto companies accuse banks of restricting competition. Summer Mersinger, CEO of the Blockchain Association, stated that Congress has an opportunity to enact bipartisan rules to protect consumers and promote innovation. The House of Representatives previously passed the Clarity Act, but the two Senate committees remain divided on revisions and voting, leading to multiple delays in hearings. The White House emphasizes that the crypto market structure bill must be passed as soon as possible. According to Crypto in America, the Senate Agriculture Committee will debate and vote on several amendments during its deliberations on Thursday, including a proposal by Senator Michael Bennet (Democrat-Colorado) that aims to establish ethical guidelines for government officials and their families holding or participating in cryptocurrency activities. Senator Amy Klobuchar (Democrat-Minnesota), senior member of the Agriculture Committee, also introduced an amendment that would delay the bill's implementation until the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has at least four confirmed commissioners, including two minority members who will be selected after consultation with the committee's senior minority members. Klobuchar's second amendment would narrow the definition of "retail participant" and clarify that the proposed Office of Digital Goods Retail Advocates is intended to protect retail customers, not to represent registered or regulated cryptocurrency companies. Other amendments include a ban on bailouts of cryptocurrency issuers, new anti-fraud provisions for cryptocurrency ATMs proposed by Senator Dick Durbin (Democrat-Illinois), and provisions restricting foreign adversaries' participation in the U.S. cryptocurrency market proposed by Senators Tommy Tuberville (Republican-Alabama) and Jerry Moran (Republican-Kansas). It is unclear how many amendments will be adopted, though some expect the deliberations to last approximately two hours. After voting on the amendments, committee members will vote on whether to send the bill to the full Senate for consideration. HSBC: Coinbase's Opposition Unlikely to Hinder US Crypto Market Structure Legislation HSBC stated that despite Coinbase's withdrawal of support for US crypto market structure legislation, the momentum for such legislation remains solid. HSBC believes the industry values ​​a long-term, clear regulatory framework more than uncertain "enforcement-style regulation." The report points out that even though Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong stated "better no legislation than bad legislation," reasonable compromises are still likely to be accepted in reality. Fairshake, the crypto political action committee co-funded by Ripple and Coinbase, is investing heavily in pushing Congress to pass clear, written regulatory rules. HSBC also anticipates that a viable path is for the Senate Agriculture Committee's bill to pass first, granting the CFTC regulatory power over the spot commodity market even if SEC-related issues are not fully resolved, providing necessary legal certainty for institutional funds to enter the market.

▌South Korea's ruling party finalizes "Digital Asset Basic Law," minimum capital requirement for stablecoin issuers approximately $3.5 million

According to South Korean media reports, South Korea's ruling Democratic Party has finalized the name of its bill aimed at regulating the virtual asset market as the "Digital Asset Basic Law" and plans to submit it before the New Year holiday. They have also agreed to set the minimum legal capital requirement for stablecoin issuers at 5 billion won (approximately $3.5 million). However, sensitive issues such as the scope of authority of the Bank of Korea and restrictions on major shareholder holdings will be finalized after further coordination with the policy committee.

Blockchain Applications

▌Coinbase Launches Prediction Market in All 50 US States via Kalshi

Coinbase announced that through a partnership with Kalshi, a prediction market platform regulated by the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), it has expanded its prediction market service to all 50 US states, completing a full launch after previous limited testing. This feature allows users to trade on the outcomes of real-world events such as politics, sports, entertainment, and economic indicators, with a minimum trade size of $1 (USD or USDC), and can be managed uniformly within the same interface as crypto assets, stocks, and cash accounts. This full rollout comes at a time of rapid growth in prediction market trading volume and revenue. Coinbase stated that the prediction market is an important part of its transformation into a "full-service exchange." Last month, Coinbase also acquired The Clearing Company to accelerate related business expansion.

▌CNBC: Nvidia and Amazon May Participate in OpenAI's Current Funding Round

According to CNBC reporter David Faber: Nvidia (NVDA.O) and Amazon (AMZN.O) may participate in OpenAI's current funding round.

▌WisdomTree Expands its Tokenized Fund to the Solana Blockchain

According to Coindesk, New York-based asset management firm WisdomTree has expanded its tokenized fund to the Solana blockchain, driving multi-chain development. Institutional and individual investors can issue, trade, and hold tokenized funds on Solana through WisdomTree Connect and WisdomTree Prime.

▌Crypto PAC Fairshake's Cash Reserves Increase to $193 Million

Fairshake, a major political action committee in the crypto industry, announced that Ripple and Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) have recently added a combined $49 million in donations, bringing its available funds for this year's US midterm congressional elections to $193 million, exceeding the size of the previous congressional election. This super PAC supported more than 50 pro-crypto candidates in the 2024 cycle and became one of the top-funded PACs in the US at the time. With the Senate about to consider the Crypto Market Structure Act, Washington has clearly felt the political influence of this cash reserve; Fairshake stated it will oppose anti-crypto stances and support pro-crypto lawmakers.

... Meanwhile, other PACs in the industry are also expanding their resources, coupled with prediction market assessments of congressional control, making the prospects for crypto legislation more speculative. According to Onchain Lens monitoring, BlackRock deposited 1,156.87 BTC (worth approximately $104 million) and 19,644 ETH (worth approximately $59.23 million) into Coinbase, and may deposit more. Strive: Holding 13,131.82 Bitcoins, Becoming the World's Tenth Largest Corporate Bitcoin Holder Strive, a US-listed Bitcoin treasury company, announced that it has completed the issuance and oversubscription of SATA shares, and simultaneously completed the Semler note exchange. To date, the company has purchased 333.89 Bitcoins at an average price of $89,851, and currently holds 13,131.82 Bitcoins, making Strive the world's tenth largest corporate Bitcoin holder. Liquid Capital founder Yi Lihua, in an article published on the X platform, stated that companies like Tether and Binance are like pumps and dumps in the industry, raking in billions of dollars in profits annually. They achieved rapid, unregulated growth in the early days of the cryptocurrency market without needing compliance. Tether, as a non-compliant stablecoin company, not only rakes in huge profits in the cryptocurrency market but also uses those profits to aggressively purchase gold. From an investment perspective, it's successful; from the perspective of industry building and respect, it's a failure. Conversely, compliant stablecoins like USD1, which use profits to subsidize users through WLFI while continuously buying up industry assets like ETH, are truly building the industry. I hope that if CZ truly believes in the supercycle and Yi Jie (a popular online figure) is optimistic about the arrival of the Bitcoin era, they should use some of the profits from Binance to buy BTC/ETH, etc. This would truly be consistent in word and deed, genuinely "keeping building" and earning them more respect. Of course, I also hope other platforms follow suit. Only an industry bull market can make everyone's lives better, while those big short sellers are the real cancer in the industry.

▌KBC Group, Belgium's second-largest bank, has increased its holdings in Strategy by 88,501 shares, bringing its total holdings to 311,624 shares (approximately US$50.44 million).

According to market news: KBC Group, Belgium's second-largest bank, has increased its holdings in Bitcoin treasury company Strategy by 88,501 shares, bringing its total holdings to 311,624 shares (approximately US$50.44 million).

Mirae Asset Global Investments Increases Stake in MSTR by 87,000 Shares Mirae Asset Global Investments, with $729 billion in assets under management, has increased its stake in Bitcoin treasury firm Strategy (MSTR) to 87,531 shares, worth $14.1 million. Coinbase Adds Hyperliquid (HYPE) to Listing Roadmap Coinbase has added Hyperliquid (HYPE) to its listing roadmap. Coinbase to Launch Moonbirds (BIRB) Spot Trading Coinbase will launch Moonbirds (BIRB) spot trading. The BIRB-USD trading pair will open later if liquidity conditions are met.

Important Economic Developments

▌Fed Holds Rates Steady, Milan and Waller Vote Against

The Federal Reserve decided to keep interest rates unchanged by a 10-2 vote. Federal Reserve Governors Milan and Waller dissented from the rate decision, supporting a 25 basis point cut.

▌Fed Raises Assessment of U.S. Economic Activity, Says It's Expanding at a "Solid" Pace

The Federal Reserve raised its assessment of economic activity, saying it is expanding at a "solid" pace. The Fed stated that the unemployment rate showed some signs of stabilization, but job growth remained subdued. The Fed stated that inflation remained slightly high. The Fed also removed the statement about increased downside risks to employment that appeared in the previous three statements.

The Federal Reserve reiterated its statement on its long-term goals and monetary policy strategy.

▌Powell: Most of the higher-than-expected inflation is from tariffs, not demand

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell stated that a significant portion of the impact of tariffs has already been transmitted through the U.S. economy. Tariffs may cause a one-off price increase. Most of the higher-than-expected inflation is from tariffs, not demand. Core PCE, excluding the impact of tariffs on goods, is slightly above 2%. The impact of tariffs on goods is expected to peak this year and then decline. If this happens, it will indicate that the Fed can ease policy. Downside risks to the labor market must also be monitored.

▌Betsent: Trump's Fed Chair nominee may be announced in about a week

U.S. Treasury Secretary Besent stated that Trump's Fed Chair nominee may be announced in about a week.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in March is 86.5%

According to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by March is 13.5%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 86.5%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by April is 24.1%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 74%, and the probability of cumulatively cutting interest rates by 50 basis points is 2.0%.

▌The S&P 500 index breaks through the 7,000-point mark for the first time

The S&P 500 index touched 7,000 points for the first time, marking a breakthrough of an important psychological level for the index.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌How AI in Cryptocurrency Trading Reshapes and Disrupts the Role of Humans

Artificial intelligence (AI) is gradually integrating into cryptocurrency trading, accelerating the analysis, execution, and optimization processes previously performed by humans. Investors and trading firms are facing the challenge of automating decision-making without diminishing control, accountability, or human judgment. While some projects are striving to build more autonomous trading systems, most AI tools in the cryptocurrency space remain subject to numerous limitations. Humans are still responsible for strategy development, setting risk limits, and bearing responsibility for outcomes, while machines handle most of the bandwidth used for data-intensive tasks such as research and monitoring. In the cryptocurrency market, the balance between automation and regulation is quietly reshaping trading workflows and beginning to redefine which human roles remain important.

AI hasn't eliminated humans from the cryptocurrency space, but it is reshaping the way labor is distributed across the entire industry.

AI hasn't removed humans from the cryptocurrency space, but it is reshaping how labor is distributed across the entire industry.

This shift has largely occurred quietly, manifesting at the task level, particularly in research roles that previously relied on teams of junior analysts and interns. As trade execution becomes increasingly automated, traders can focus their energy on strategy and risk control rather than manual operations.

data.title: The New York Stock Exchange completely overturned the traditional closing model.
data.description:
Written by: Vaidik Mandloi & Translated by: Block unicorn

Foreword

Last week, the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) announced plans to build a 24/7 blockchain-based tokenized securities trading platform. At first glance, this seems like just another headline about "traditional finance adopting blockchain." For those who have been following the cryptocurrency space for the past few years, tokenized stocks, on-chain settlement, and stablecoin funding are already familiar concepts.

However, this announcement is not intended to test new technologies, but rather to challenge market sectors that have rarely changed.

The stock market still uses fixed trading hours and delayed settlement mechanisms, primarily because this system has effectively managed risk for decades. Trading takes place within a short time window, with clearing and settlement occurring afterward. Large amounts of capital remain idle between trading and settlement to absorb counterparty risk.

While this system is stable, it suffers from slow speed, high costs, and increasing disconnect from global capital flows. The New York Stock Exchange's proposed solution directly challenges this structure, reshaping how markets process time. A trading venue that never closes, settlement times closer to execution times, fewer periods of price stagnation while risk exposure remains—all pointing in the same direction. Unlike cryptocurrency markets built under varying constraints, traditional stock markets can suspend trading or delay settlement. In contrast, cryptocurrency markets operate continuously—pricing, execution, and settlement occur in real time, reflecting risk immediately rather than being delayed. While this design has its own shortcomings, it eliminates the inefficiencies of the time-based systems that traditional markets still rely on. The New York Stock Exchange is currently attempting to integrate elements of a continuous trading model into a regulated environment while retaining safeguards that maintain stock market stability. This article will delve into the actual operation of the New York Stock Exchange and why it's more than just a headline story. Why This Isn't "Just Another Tokenization Announcement" The NYSE announcement doesn't focus on stock tokenization itself. Stock tokenization has existed in various forms for years, but most have failed. What makes this announcement different is who initiated the tokenization and what level they're targeting. Past attempts at tokenized stocks aimed to replicate stocks outside the core market, such as FTX's tokenized stocks, Securitize's tokenized equity products, and synthetic equity products built on protocols like Mirror and Synthetix. These products traded in different market venues and at different times, relying on price data from markets that were frequently closed. Therefore, they struggled to achieve sustained liquidity and were mostly used as niche access products rather than core market instruments. These early attempts all occurred outside the primary stock market. They did not change how stocks were issued, how they were traded and settled, or how risk management was managed within the actual pricing system. However, the New York Stock Exchange is addressing this issue internally. Instead of launching parallel products, it is adjusting trading and settlement methods within a regulated exchange. The securities themselves remain unchanged, but their trading and settlement methods will change over time. The most important part of this announcement is the decision to combine continuous trading with on-chain settlement. Either of these changes could be implemented independently. The NYSE could have extended trading hours without introducing blockchain, or experimented with token issuance without affecting trading hours. However, the NYSE ultimately chose to link the two together. This indicates that the NYSE's focus is not on the convenience of trading or user experience, but on how risk exposure and capital operate when the market is running continuously. A large part of today's market infrastructure was built to address the so-called "time lag." When the market closes, trading stops, but positions remain open. Even if prices no longer move, risk and exposure persist. To manage these time lags, brokers and clearinghouses need collateral and safety buffers that remain locked until settlement is complete. While this process is stable, its efficiency decreases as market trading speeds increase, global participation grows, and more trading activity occurs outside of local trading hours. Continuous market operation and faster trade settlement can bridge this gap. Risk is addressed as it arises, rather than being held overnight or over several days. This doesn't eliminate risk, but it reduces the length of time capital is idle simply to cover time uncertainty. This is also the problem the NYSE is working to solve. This is also why stablecoin-based financing methods are integrated into this model. Currently, cash and securities circulate through different systems and often follow different timelines, leading to delays and additional coordination work. Using on-chain cash allows both parties to trade simultaneously, without waiting for external payment systems. Combined with continuous trading, this is crucial for global markets where information and investors are active 24/7. Prices can adjust in real time when news is released, instead of adjusting hours later when the next market opens, as was previously the case. However, it remains unclear whether this will improve market performance under pressure, which is precisely where the real significance of these changes lies. Changes within the Market One simple yet important consequence of the New York Stock Exchange's proposed solution lies in the way trade clearing and settlement are handled behind the scenes. Today's stock market heavily relies on net settlement. Millions of trades are offset against each other before settlement, reducing the cash and collateral required for transactions. This approach works well within systems based on fixed trading hours and delayed settlement, but it also relies on time differences for efficiency. Continuous trading and faster settlement speeds are changing how trades are cleared. When trades settle faster, the opportunity to offset large volumes of trading activity through end-of-day netting decreases. This means some of the efficiency gains from bulk trading is reduced. Therefore, brokers, clearing members, and liquidity providers need to manage funds and risk exposure throughout the trading day, rather than relying on overnight settlement processes to absorb and diversify risk. Market makers and large intermediaries will be the first to adapt to this change. Under the current model, they could hold inventory and adjust positions based on predictable settlement cycles. As settlement speeds increase and trading continues, position turnover accelerates, requiring funds to be available more quickly. Companies that have adopted automation, real-time risk checks, and flexible liquidity are better positioned to handle this situation. Others face stricter constraints due to less time to rebalance positions or rely on overnight settlements. Short selling and securities lending face similar pressures. Currently, borrowing shares, locating inventory, and resolving settlement issues typically involve multiple steps and time windows. Shorter settlement deadlines compress these steps, making it harder to postpone settlement failures, and borrowing costs and availability adapt to market changes more quickly. Crucially, most of the impact is behind the scenes. Retail users may not notice significant changes at the interface level, but institutions providing liquidity and funding positions face stricter time constraints. Some friction points are eliminated, while others become more difficult to ignore. Time no longer allows for error correction as much as before; systems must remain synchronized throughout the trading day, rather than adjusting afterward. The ensuing second-order effect: Once markets no longer rely on time as a buffer, a different set of constraints begins to take effect. This is first reflected in how capital is reused within large institutions. Today, the same balance sheet can support positions across multiple settlement cycles because debt eventually offsets itself over time. But as settlement cycles tighten, this reuse becomes more difficult. Capital must be in place earlier and more precisely, subtly altering internal capital allocation decisions, limiting leverage, and changing how liquidity is priced during market volatility. Another consequence is the way volatility propagates. In batch-based markets, risk tends to accumulate during market closures and then be released at predictable moments such as opening or closing. When trading and settlement continue, this clustering effect no longer applies. Price volatility is spread across the entire time frame, rather than concentrated in a specific time window. This won't make markets calmer, but it does make volatility more difficult to predict and manage, and harder to handle with old strategies that rely on pauses, resets, or downtime. It also affects coordination between different markets. Currently, a significant portion of price discovery is not conducted through major stock exchanges, but through futures, ETFs, and other proxy instruments, primarily because the underlying markets are closed. When major exchanges remain open and accelerate settlement, the importance of these workarounds diminishes. Arbitrage opportunities will flow back to major markets, altering derivatives liquidity patterns and reducing the need to hedge risk through indirect instruments. Finally, this changes the role of exchanges themselves. Exchanges are no longer merely order setters but are more involved in risk coordination. This increases their responsibility in stress events and shortens the gap between trading infrastructure and risk management. In summary, these effects explain why this move is crucial even if it doesn't immediately change the face or atmosphere of the market. The effects will gradually emerge, manifesting in how capital is reused, how volatility spreads over time, the shift of arbitrage activities to major trading venues, and how balance sheets are managed under stricter constraints. These are not short-term improvements or superficial upgrades, but structural changes that reshape the system's internal incentive mechanisms. Once markets begin to operate in this way, reversing these changes will be far more difficult than adopting them in the first place. In today's market structure, delays and multi-layered intermediaries act as a buffer when problems arise, allowing issues to surface later, losses to be absorbed gradually, and responsibility to be distributed across time and institutions. However, as timelines shorten, this buffering effect begins to weaken. Funding and risk decisions are increasingly closer to the execution phase. There is less room to cover up mistakes or postpone consequences, so failures will surface earlier and are easier to trace. The New York Stock Exchange is testing whether a large, regulated market can function properly under these conditions without relying on delayed trading to manage risk. Shorter time between trades and settlement means less room for adjusting positions, diversifying funds, or post-trade adjustments. This change forces problems to surface during normal trading, rather than deferred to later processes, thus clearly exposing weaknesses in the market.

                Jinse Finance reports that the Bitcoin ZK-Rollup project Citrea officially launched its mainnet on January 27th. Citrea positions itself as an application layer specifically designed for the Bitcoin capital market. This layer aims to introduce programmable applications secured by the security of the Bitcoin network while maintaining liquidity anchored to Bitcoin itself. At the start of the mainnet launch, the network introduced cBTC—a Bitcoin-backed asset usable in various decentralized applications—and ctUSD—a native stablecoin used to support on-chain liquidity. According to Citrea, cBTC combines zero-knowledge proofs (ZK) with a BitVM-based verification mechanism, reducing reliance on custodians or multi-signature trust structures. As long as at least one honest participant exists, any fraudulent activity can be challenged on the Bitcoin mainnet. This model aims to provide higher security than previous Bitcoin cross-chain bridge designs.
