By enabling discovery mechanisms and portable reputation, ERC-8004 allows AI agents to interact between different organizations, ensuring reputation flows everywhere, thereby unlocking a global AI service interoperability market.

                      5. Zora content token surge on Base exposes "vanity metric" problem

                      Jinse Finance reports that the number of tokens issued on the Layer 2 public chain Base has recently surged dramatically, exceeding 100,000 in a single day, almost entirely driven by Zora's content token mechanism.

                      Zora allows creators to mint tokens at virtually zero cost, but network activity addresses and transaction volume are at an 18-month low, indicating a lack of real economic value for these tokens. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Is it time to enter the market for MSTR after the premium reset?
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

While Bitcoin prices have retreated 30% from their all-time highs and spot ETFs have been experiencing net outflows for three consecutive months, Strategy, the world's largest Bitcoin strategic reserve company, has bucked the trend by aggressively adding over $3.7 billion in a single month. Simultaneously, its stock, MSTR, has undergone a sharp correction of over 60%, and its mNAV has been lowered from 2.4 to a more rational range of 1.07. This article will analyze three future scenarios for Strategy under different market conditions, combining macroeconomic data and the current weak institutional environment. With the premium bubble bursting, has it arrived at the best entry point for MSTR?

Strategy bucks the trend, buying over $3.7 billion worth of BTC in a single month. After reaching an all-time high of $126,000 in October 2025, Bitcoin experienced a sharp pullback, currently hovering around $88,000, a drop of 30%. However, this correction did not deter Strategy, the world's largest Bitcoin reserve holder. In early 2026, the company continued its aggressive buying spree, demonstrating its strong confidence in Bitcoin to the global market. According to data from Bitcoin Strategy Tracker (https://saylortracker.com/?tab=home), Strategy purchased over 40,000 Bitcoins in January 2026 alone. In particular, within just two weeks from January 6th to January 20th, Strategy purchased a total of 35,932 Bitcoins in two batches, with an average purchase price ranging from $91,500 to $95,300. Statistics show that Strategy's total investment in January reached a staggering $3.7 billion; as of January 27th, its balance sheet showed a cumulative holding of 712,647 Bitcoins, with an overall average purchase cost of $76,038. Besides its aggressive buying spree in the spot market, Strategy is also actively expanding its influence into the European market. Several financial institutions have launched leveraged products linked to Strategy (MSTR) on major European exchanges, attempting to attract traditional capital from across the Atlantic. On the other hand, top global financial institutions have shown a significant increase in interest in holding MSTR. Vanguard Group, an asset management giant, recently disclosed holding Strategy stock; the Louisiana Employees Retirement Fund in the United States and Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Group, a Japanese financial giant, have also recently reported including MSTR in their portfolios. However, despite the enthusiastic buying of Strategy and numerous positive news, the overall market chill has not been dispelled. For investors, current macroeconomic data reveals a clear demand gap: Bitcoin spot ETFs have experienced net outflows for three consecutive months: Since November 2025, US Bitcoin spot ETFs have been in a three-month period of capital outflows, including a net outflow of $3.48 billion in November and $1.09 billion in December. Although the outflow rate slowed to $111 million in January 2026, the outflow trend has not ended. This shows that mainstream Wall Street funds have been continuously withdrawing during this period, in stark contrast to Strategy's contrarian increase in investment.

Spatial and Uneven Inflows of Bitcoin Treasury Funds: Glassnode report points out that recent corporate fund inflows are mainly concentrated in individual event-driven transactions, rather than widespread fund accumulation. The chart below shows that buying is primarily strategy-driven, while other Bitcoin treasury companies have not made further purchases recently, a stark contrast to the period from May to August 2025 when multiple treasury companies coordinated their purchases, accelerating the trend.

Furthermore, a harsh phenomenon can be observed: Bitcoin has fallen from a high of $126,000 to its current price of $88,000, a drop of approximately 30%; however, Strategy (MSTR)'s stock price has plummeted from a high of $457 in July of last year to approximately $160 currently, a drop of a staggering 65%, meaning Strategy's decline is almost twice that of Bitcoin. The leverage that was a powerful tool during the initial upward trend has become an "accelerator" for the stock price crash during this downward cycle.

...

MSTR Premium Reset and Three Endgames

After experiencing a double correction in both stock price and premium, the core question facing investors is: Is Strategy's current MSTR stock price of $160 a buying opportunity, or the beginning of another crash?

A key indicator is Strategy's mNAV performance relative to its Bitcoin holdings.

At the end of 2024, the market was willing to pay a premium of up to 2.4 times to hold MSTR; however, with the recent stock price decline of over 60%, this premium has shrunk significantly to 1.07 times. From a valuation perspective, this means that the emotional bubble in the market has been significantly squeezed, and the current stock price is very close to its asset floor, providing a more cost-effective entry point for long-term Bitcoin bulls. However, Strategy's debt risk cannot be ignored. As of early 2026, the company's perpetual preferred equity reached $8.36 billion, exceeding its $8.21 billion convertible debt. While this conversion eliminates the refinancing pressure of maturing debt, it also brings continuous cash outflows. Strategy currently holds approximately $2.25 billion in cash reserves, while its annual dividend and interest obligations are approximately $876 million. At the current rate of expenditure, the company has approximately two and a half years of financial buffer. A more critical test will come in September 2027, when the $1.01 billion "sell option" on its 2028 notes may require a cash payment, depending on the share price performance at that time.

At the same time, we can combine Forbes' views to summarize the following three scenarios for the future market:

Scenario 1 (Basic Assumption): If Bitcoin maintains a sideways trend between $85,000 and $100,000, the market will enter a period of patience testing.

Scenario 1 (Basic Assumption): If Bitcoin maintains a sideways trend between $85,000 and $100,000, the market will enter a period of patience testing.

... Limited by macroeconomic policy uncertainty, geopolitical instability, and the lack of new large institutional buying groups, Strategy's premium is expected to remain low, with the stock price expected to fluctuate between $150 and $250. Scenario Two (Optimistic Outlook): If Bitcoin breaks through the $100,000 mark and challenges $150,000, Strategy is expected to reignite its "leveraged long" momentum. According to analysis by Canaccord Genuity and Bernstein (see https://www.quiverquant.com/news/MicroStrategyStockOpinionsonRecentBitcoinAcquisition), the target price could return to above $450. For investors seeking excess returns, Strategy remains the most powerful leveraged Bitcoin investment in the current capital market.

Scenario 3 (Pessimistic Expectation): This is the scenario most feared by critics like Peter Schiff. If the price of Bitcoin falls below $80,000, or even approaches the average cost line of $76,000, the company will find it difficult to maintain its buying power through low-cost financing tools; more seriously, the accumulated dividends from perpetual preferred shares will also face enormous financial pressure, potentially triggering a vicious cycle where asset value and financing capacity mutually undermine each other.

Market "consensus" is in a fragile period; whether or not to go long depends on investors' risk appetite.

Should we go long on Strategy (MSTR) now?

The argument for going long is that the premium has been reset (mNAV has dropped from a high of $2.4 to $1.07), and the risk has been largely released. If one agrees that Bitcoin below $100,000 represents a serious "value mismatch," then Strategy is the best leverage to amplify profits. A more conservative view is that macroeconomic policy uncertainty, geopolitical turmoil, continued outflows from ETFs, and corporate hesitancy indicate that market consensus is fragile. Strategy alone cannot withstand the overall macroeconomic pull; it may only provide a floor. Ultimately, the answer depends on the investor's risk appetite. For those who believe gold will eventually prevail, this is an entry opportunity after the premium has been reduced; however, for those seeking stability, remaining neutral may be a more rational choice until Wall Street institutions resume net inflows.

data.title: The Game at the Regulatory Crossroads: The Covert Battle Between Robinhood and Coinbase
data.description:
Author: Daniel Li

2026At the beginning of the year, the US crypto industry ushered in a key turning point—the Clarity Act for Digital Asset Markets was delayed during the Senate review stage. This was supposed to be a systemic project to "end regulatory chaos," but unexpectedly it ignited the sharpest divisions within the industry.2026 Two of the most representative cryptocurrency exchanges in the US stock market—Coinbase and Robinhood—have unusually taken opposing sides: the former publicly opposes the current version of the bill, while the latter calls for the legislation to be passed as soon as possible. On the surface, this appears to be a debate over regulatory attitudes; but at a deeper level, it represents a direct clash between two business models, two growth paths, and two visions of the future of crypto. For ordinary crypto investors, this is not only about policy direction, but also about where you will trade, how you will make money, what risks you will bear, and whether crypto assets will become the "next-generation financial infrastructure" or just an "ordinary investment category." Part One: Why Does a Bill Affect the Nerves of the Entire Crypto Market? For over a decade, US crypto investors have lived in a peculiar institutional situation: they can buy coins, trade, and arbitrage, but they have never been able to confirm whether the market they are in is "part of the legitimate financial system." The same token might be considered a security by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), but a commodity by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). Different states have different licensing requirements; the same business can operate in New York but might be shut down in California. Companies often only realize they've crossed a line after receiving investigation letters, lawyer's letters, or even fines. The market isn't governed by rules, but driven by litigation. For ordinary investors, this ambiguity in the regulatory framework is not an abstract issue, but rather part of everyday risks: Will a certain token be delisted? Will a certain platform suddenly freeze its functions? Will stablecoin yields be suspended? These are not market fluctuations, but rather "regulatory risks" directly brought about by policy uncertainty. The CLARITY Act was proposed against this backdrop. Its initial intention is not complicated, nor is it simply to "strengthen regulation," but rather to attempt to solve a long-standing unresolved issue: who should regulate crypto assets, and by what logic? The bill plans to classify digital assets into three categories at the federal level: security, commodity, and utility. It will also redefine the authority boundaries between the SEC and the CFTC, establish a unified national compliance framework for trading platforms, and provide clear legal definitions for new forms such as stablecoins, DeFi, and tokenized assets. In other words, it wants to end the gray order of "develop first, then sue, then supplement the rules".

2025At the end of the year, the bill has passed a preliminary version in the House of Representatives; 2026 text="">21Robinhood text="">The size of funded accounts has reached approximately2600–2700Ten thousand, andthose who prefer to obtain unified, clear, and replicable national rules as soon as possible will transform crypto trading into a standardized and scalable basic function, just like stock trading.

Therefore,CLARITY Actis not just a regulatory act, but also an "institutional lever" that determines the success or failure of a business model.

If regulations tighten, stablecoin yields will be limited, DeFi will contract, and tokens will be fully securitized, significantly compressing Coinbase's long-term strategic space. Conversely, if regulations focus on clarifying boundaries, reducing cross-state compliance costs, and encouraging the adoption of standardized financial products, Robinhood's user expansion speed and business replication capabilities will be significantly amplified. However, it should be noted that although Coinbase and Robinhood have significant differences in their specific terms, at a higher level, their goals are not contradictory: they both hope to end the long-standing regulatory gray area through legislation, making the legal attributes, business boundaries, and compliance responsibilities of crypto assets clear and predictable. For Coinbase, this means institutional clients and long-term capital can enter the market with greater peace of mind; for Robinhood, it means that crypto trading can be standardized and scaled up to a wider retail audience, just like stocks. It is precisely because of their different paths and benefit structures that the two companies have formed a public confrontation over the same bill—they are not vying for short-term stock price fluctuations, but for the most core asset in the future US crypto market: the entry point for funds from tens of millions of ordinary investors, and the financial service ecosystem built around this entry point. The final outcome of this competition will not only determine the winner and loser of the platforms, but will also profoundly change the role and way of life of ordinary investors in the crypto market. Part Three: What Will Ordinary Crypto Investors Face After the Regulatory Turning Point? The power struggle surrounding the CLARITY bill, while seemingly between Congress and company executives, ultimately benefits ordinary investors. Once the regulatory framework is implemented, it will not only change the business boundaries of platforms but also reshape individual investment methods, return structures, and risk profiles. The most obvious change is that the market will shift from "free growth" to "rule-based selection." For the past decade or so, the core characteristics of the crypto market have been low barriers to entry, numerous projects, and rapid pace: any team could issue a token, and any platform could list it for trading. Investors faced a highly open, but equally chaotic, market. The CLARITY bill requires assets to clearly define their attributes and accept corresponding information disclosure and registration regulations. This means that many small and medium-sized projects will gradually exit mainstream platforms, reducing the number of assets available to investors, but increasing overall transparency and verifiability. The practical result of this is that the space for Ponzi schemes and "air coins" to survive is being compressed, but the probability of "100x coins" and "1000x coins" is also decreasing. The market will become more predictable and less likely to offer opportunities for huge profits. The second change is a fundamental shift in the nature of crypto accounts. In the past, many investors viewed crypto accounts as a "second account" outside the traditional financial system: free transfers, ambiguous tax implications, and minimal record-keeping. However, with clearer regulations, crypto accounts will increasingly resemble securities accounts: transaction records will be automatically generated, tax declarations will be synchronized, fund flows will be traceable, and accounts will be subject to compliance reviews at any time. Coinbase has integrated with tax systems in multiple jurisdictions, and Robinhood is also incorporating crypto accounts into its securities account system. For investors, this means a necessary shift in mindset: crypto assets are no longer considered "gray assets outside the regulatory framework," but are now formally incorporated into the regulatory network. The third change occurs in the revenue structure itself. In the past, most retail investors' profit logic boiled down to one sentence: buy low and sell high. However, in a more clearly defined regulatory environment, the financial attributes of the crypto market will continue to strengthen, and stablecoin interest, staking yields, platform wealth management, and structured products will gradually become important components. Coinbase is increasing its stake in USDC and USDC, and is also testing yield accounts. This means that crypto investment will shift from "betting on direction" to "managing assets." Yield curves will be smoother, volatility will decrease, but get-rich-quick returns will become increasingly scarce. Meanwhile, risks will not disappear, only change form. Project collapses and exchange misappropriation of funds may decrease, but regulatory risks will become new variables: tokens may be suddenly classified as securities and trading suspended, certain products may be delisted due to compliance issues, accounts may be frozen under scrutiny, and cross-platform transfers may be restricted. These risks no longer stem from the projects themselves, but from the adjustment and enforcement of rules. Investors need to pay attention to more than just stock charts and narratives; they also need to consider policy trends, regulatory statements, and platform announcements. Ultimately, an unavoidable trend is that crypto investing is becoming increasingly like traditional finance, and increasingly "boring." It will gradually become a standard option in asset allocation, rather than a machine for creating myths. Robinhood hopes to become a universal investment tool, while Coinbase hopes it will become a core component of the next generation of financial infrastructure. However, both paths ultimately converge—crypto assets will eventually be integrated into the mainstream system.

data.title: Your mindset focused on issuing tokens is the real culprit preventing you from building a great protocol.
data.description:
Author: danny Source: X, @agintender

I typed my first line of smart contract code during the 2017 ICO frenzy. As a veteran builder who has lived to 2026, experiencing the "September 4th" crackdown, the DeFi Summer, the NFT craze, the FTX crash, and countless "crypto is dead" pronouncements, I've seen countless projects "kill" themselves in a million ways.

I've discovered that in this long Crypto era, the definition of "winning" has never been how many billions your TGE FDV reached on a single day, but rather the frequency of token usage after it's unlocked.

Every team with a little technical background or resources can issue a token at least once. Even if the code is forked, even if the white paper is written by GPT, as long as they're riding the wave of a bull market, anyone can become a short-lived "unicorn."

So, for project teams, what exactly does "winning" mean? It means that you've issued your token, and years later, your protocol is still running, and your contract is still generating real interactions, instead of just a bunch of bots artificially inflating trading volume. In other words, if you want to change the world through Web3, you must first realize that this isn't a competition of "who raises the most funds" or "who gets the most token listings," but a marathon of "whose ecosystem can withstand the test of decentralization." I. When we say "crypto is finished," what are we talking about? During every bear market, if you're seeking funding or going home for the Chinese New Year, you'll always hear these kinds of comments: "Is your industry dead?" "I see US stocks are hitting new highs, why are yours still falling?" "Is this only used in industrial parks?" "Precious metals have gone crazy? You're still standing still?" If, as a Builder, you feel ashamed when you hear these comments, or try to explain, "We're building Layer 3 to improve TPS," then you can leave. You don't understand why you're here. Are we competing to see who can outgrow gold, crude oil, and Nvidia? If you're after efficiency in asset appreciation, why don't you change your name to Trump? Buy a Nasdaq ETF? The Seven Sisters? They're not only more stable, but also legally protected! Are we competing on who can write data faster? Don't be ridiculous. For efficiency, centralized products crush everything. Alipay's TPS is ten thousand times that of Ethereum; AWS's cost is one hundred millionth of on-chain storage. To put it bluntly, the future of decentralization, developed over 10 years, still relies on AWS. If your initial goal in creating Web3 was to build a "more efficient" internet? Or to bring traditional liquidity onto the blockchain? Then you've lost from day one. Every resurgence of Crypto, or rather, Satoshi Nakamoto's initial intention in writing the genesis block, was never for "faster, cheaper." It was for rebellion. It was for rebellion against the world's growing inequality. It's to resist the power to arbitrarily freeze your bank accounts. It's to resist the tech giants who sell the data you create without giving you a single penny. It's to resist the central banks that recklessly print money and dilute the fruits of your labor. The so-called "inefficiency" (Gas fees, private key management, node confirmation) is a tax we must pay for "fairness" and "sovereignty." As a project developer, you must be acutely aware that your product must possess a feature that centralized giants absolutely cannot provide—censorship resistance and independent sovereignty. If the answer is "no," it means your users are only speculating, and you are merely an inefficient alternative. If the answer is "yes," it means you have built a truly decentralized path. Because Tencent cannot promise never to ban accounts, but you can. Because banks cannot promise 24-hour cross-border transfers that are instant and unblocked, but you can. Because game companies can modify drop rates at any time, but your smart contracts cannot. So, Builders, stop worrying about TPS. Think about how to return power to users and incorporate open source into anti-monopoly code. This is the only battlefield where you can win against the Web2 giants; this is the meaning of blockchain. If it's for freedom, both can be sacrificed. If you're not fighting for freedom, what battlefield are you on? II. True death is not a drop in coin price, but the collapse of the "vanity indicator." Understanding this underlying logic, you'll see that most projects are "slowly committing suicide" in the current market. Whenever I see founders boasting at conferences: "Our TVL has surpassed $1 billion!" "Our daily active addresses are number one across the entire network!" "Our transaction volume is the highest across the entire network!" Only two words usually flash through my mind: It's almost here. It's almost time for a collapse. If you've weathered enough bull and bear markets, you'll see a clear pattern: Data built on "sweeteners/bribes/incentives" is never a moat, but a time bomb. The stagnation of crypto projects is essentially the disastrous consequence of "incentive mismatch." More precisely, a project is not far from its demise when the following three things happen simultaneously: Token distribution is complete (or the high-inflation model fails); Real external revenue is zero; Current consensus cannot explain "why I should hold this governance token." In such a situation, the price collapse is not because the market is shorting you, but because your only "product" is your token, and now that product is unsaleable. This is why some project teams are obsessed with market capitalization management, trying to draw up the future with candlestick charts and getting influential figures to endorse their projects, only to find themselves working for market makers and exchanges. Please note: TVL is absolutely not equivalent to protocol value. TVL is the money temporarily deposited in your pool; it's there to make money, not to consume. If you can't provide opportunities to make money, it will leave. Protocol value is the cost (Gas, fees, premium) that users pay to solve problems. TVL is bought, protocol value is earned. TVL attracts attention, protocol value sustains life. Only TVL without revenue → Ponzi scheme. Only revenue without TVL → Small and beautiful tool. When both are present → True infrastructure. III. Mercenary Capital vs. Missionary Users: Who is your "core base"? I know what you're thinking. "Old Lamp, I understand what you're saying, but if we don't implement points-based tasks and high APY (Average Per Year), nobody will come! How do we get a cold start?" This brings us to the core pain point of Web3 startups: Are the people you recruit family who treat you like family, or robbers who see you as a wallet? You need to understand two very different groups of people: 1. The Mercenaries They are a group of extremely rational profit-seekers within the Pump and Dump cult. (Yes, we're talking about you!) Characteristics: Large capital, sensitive to slippage, no loyalty, uses scripts. Behavior: They go wherever the APY is high. You give them subsidies, they come; you stop subsidizing, they withdraw their funds faster than the speed of light. Your Misconception: You think they're using your product. No, they're mining your cryptocurrency. You think those impressive daily active user numbers are growth, but actually you're selling your company equity (tokens) at a low price. 2. Missionaries: They are the people who genuinely identify with your narrative, culture, or technological vision. Characteristics: They may not have large sums of money, have high tolerance, spontaneously answer questions in the community, and defend you on Twitter. Behavior: They use your product because it's "easy to use" or "cool," not just because "it's profitable." Value: They are your moat. These are the people still discussing product improvements on Discord when the price of the token has dropped by 90%. While both are mercenaries, if you want to survive in this cruel dark forest, you must learn to: utilize mercenaries to serve missionaries. Look at the difference between Blur and Uniswap: Blur, through extremely aggressive points airdrops, drained the liquidity of the NFT market in the short term. Was it successful? Judging from transaction volume, yes. But essentially, it turned NFTs from "collectibles" into "financial derivatives." User retention depends entirely on the expectation of "next quarter's airdrop" (doesn't that sound a lot like these Perp Dexes?). This is a classic case of "paying for mercenaries." Uniswap, for a long time, didn't even have a token. People used it because it truly solved the problem of "decentralized trading." Its code, its security, and its brand consensus have cultivated extremely strong user habits.

Three questions to prevent project teams from "leeching off" your product:

  1. If I stop all token incentives tomorrow, will anyone still use my product? (If no one, you should ask yourself if you're running a charity?)

  2. In my community, are there more people discussing "price" or "product"? (If it's all "WEN BINANCE" and "WEN Airdrop," the community is just like that.)

  3. Am I rewarding "loyalty" or "capital"?

  4. Are I rewarding "loyalty" or "capital"?

    5. (Most airdrop rules essentially reward large investors; try distributing the tokens to those who write tutorials, report bugs, and do governance.) IV. What exactly did those projects that weathered economic cycles do right? Finally, let's talk about mindset. In a bear market, watching other low-quality projects rise 100 times in a day, or XX project raising 100 million in funding, while your painstakingly written code goes unread, the loneliness and self-doubt are fatal. Based on my personal observations, those founders who survived share the following characteristics: 1. Rejecting "Pseudo-diligence" and returning to "first principles" Many project teams frantically "stir things up" during the bear market: today they're making a blockchain game based on a hot topic, tomorrow they're renaming it to AI, and the day after that they're making SocialFi. This is called "pseudo-diligence." You may seem busy, but you're actually avoiding the core issues. A true builder asks themselves: What irreplaceable problem does my protocol actually solve? If it's a DEX, it's extreme slippage and security; if it's L2, it's extreme fees and compatibility. No matter how cold the market is, as long as you solve the real problem, the value will eventually return. 2. Establishing a "transparent contract," not a "price promise" Don't promise a price. Never do it. As a project team, your commitment should be: "We will always remain transparent, we will always deliver code, and we will always maintain the security of the protocol." When the price of the coin drops, if you can honestly tell the community, "Hey, the market is terrible, our vault can last for 3 years, and we will continue to develop this feature," you will find that trust is a more expensive asset than liquidity. Consensus always cycles.

    3. Endure loneliness and enjoy the "bonus of building"

    Bull markets are the noisiest times. Investors will urge you to issue a coin, exchanges will urge you to list it, and users will urge you to pump the price. It's difficult to calm down and write a good underlying architecture. Bear markets are a gift from God to builders. Only when no one cares about you can you experiment at low cost and recruit employees who are truly passionate about technology (not speculators who come for stock options).

    ... Conclusion: To the "Fools" Left at the Table This process is extremely painful. You will be seen as an outsider, ridiculed for not keeping up with the "hot topics," and treated like a sucker by agencies. You will experience team turmoil filled with betrayal, relentless hacking attacks, and the ever-present shadow of regulatory oversight. But if you survive, you will gain more than just wealth; you will gain the power to define a new way of collaboration. The code you build will become the law of the future digital society; the mechanisms you design will become bridges of trust between strangers. The crypto world has never lacked smart people—those who are extremely rational, extremely self-interested, and ready to cash out and leave at any moment. What it lacks are "fools." It's the kind of fool who knows the road is long and arduous, knows that doing "real work" is far less lucrative than "market manipulation," yet is still willing to stay at the poker table for that sliver of possibility of changing the world. That's why I'm here. That's why you're here. The smart people in this world are all working on AI, SaaS, and quantitative trading because those are the most efficient and fastest ways to make money. Only we "fools" are stubbornly struggling with these inefficient distributed ledgers. But it is precisely this disdain for "efficiency" and obsession with "fairness" that is the spark that allows the crypto industry to be reborn from countless death sentences. If you are such a Builder, welcome to this endless marathon. Even if you are in the abyss now, please don't extinguish the torch in your hand. We'll meet at the summit at the next true "consensus upgrade."

data.title: a16z In-Depth Research Report: Is the Quantum Threat Really Coming?
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>Recently, the threat posed by quantum computing to blockchain security has once again attracted widespread attention within the industry. Wood, Global Head of Equity Strategy at Jefferies, even publicly stated that, considering the potential threat quantum computing could pose to BTC's encryption algorithms and security, he has removed 10% of his portfolio from BTC and replaced it with physical gold and gold mining stocks. Therefore, what stage has quantum computing technology reached, and will quantum computers actually pose a substantial threat to blockchain encryption algorithms? To answer these questions, we have compiled a special research report published by a top US VC firm, a16z, which provides a detailed breakdown and analysis of the issues related to quantum computing and encryption algorithms. The in-depth research report from a16z can be summarized into the following key points: 1) Many people exaggerate the reality of the quantum threat; current quantum computing technology is still in its very early stages. A truly practical quantum computer capable of breaking encryption algorithms (CRQC) is likely still a long way off in the short term. Many news reports use terms like "quantum advantage" and "logical qubits" to mislead the public and cause misjudgments about the progress. 2) Don't confuse encryption and signatures; their urgency in the face of quantum threats is completely different. Encryption (communication confidentiality) needs post-quantum processing earlier: because HNDL attacks (storing ciphertext now and decrypting it when quantum technology matures) are already a real risk. Signatures (used in blockchain for authorizing transfers) don't need to be changed across the entire network immediately: because signatures don't involve the risk of storing today and decrypting in the future; they can only be forged after quantum computers truly emerge. 3) Most blockchains are not afraid of HNDL, but Bitcoin still needs to be planned now. Non-privacy chains like BTC and ETH mainly rely on signatures, so they don't need to migrate immediately out of concern for HNDL attacks. BTC's pressure mainly comes from within: slow upgrades, migration requiring user assistance, a large number of old coins that may remain inactive for a long time, and coins with exposed public keys are very difficult to protect. Therefore, migration must be planned years in advance, rather than waiting for the actual quantum threat to approach before taking action. 4) Specific Action Recommendations Systems for long-term secure communication and key exchange should adopt post-quantum solutions as soon as possible. For public blockchains, wallets, and signature systems, there's no need to immediately switch to post-quantum solutions, but they should start designing migration routes, contingency plans, old addresses, and rules for handling lost assets now. The more realistic security focus is actually on bugs and technical vulnerabilities. Ensuring engineering security is perhaps more meaningful than worrying about quantum threats. The following is a compiled version of the full research report for your reference: The timeline for quantum computers that pose a "substantial threat to cryptography" is often exaggerated—which has fueled calls for an immediate and comprehensive shift to post-quantum cryptography. However, these calls often overlook the costs and risks of premature migration, and also ignore the starkly different risk profiles between different cryptographic primitives: Post-quantum encryption, despite its high cost, needs to be deployed as soon as possible because Harvest-now-decrypt-later (HNDL) attacks are already occurring—sensitive data encrypted today may still be valuable even decades from now when quantum computers are available. Post-quantum encryption does have performance overhead and implementation risks, but for data that needs to be kept confidential for a long time, HNDL attacks leave us with almost no choice. Post-quantum signatures, on the other hand, represent a different accounting method. It is unaffected by HNDL attacks, but its costs and risks (larger size, performance overhead, immature implementation, and vulnerabilities) mean that it should be proceeded with caution rather than migrated immediately. These distinctions are crucial. Misconceptions can distort cost-benefit analyses, causing teams to overlook more realistic and pressing security risks—such as software bugs. The real challenge in successfully migrating to post-quantum cryptography is aligning urgency with real threats. Below, I will clarify some common misconceptions about quantum threats and cryptography—covering encryption, signatures, and zero-knowledge proofs, and specifically discussing their impact on blockchain. Where are we? It is highly unlikely that a quantum computer (CRQC) posing a "substantial threat to cryptography" will emerge in the 2020s, despite some high-profile claims suggesting the opposite. Here, CRQC refers to a fault-tolerant, error-correcting quantum computer capable of running Shor's algorithm at a sufficient scale to attack elliptic curve cryptography or RSA within a reasonable timeframe (e.g., breaking secp256k1 or RSA-2048 within a maximum of about a month of continuous computation). Based on any reasonable interpretation of publicly available milestones and resource estimates, we are still very far from CRQC. Some companies claim that CRQC may emerge before 2030 or well before 2035, but publicly available progress does not support these claims. For reference: Of all current mainstream approaches—ion traps, superconducting qubits, neutral atom systems—no platform today comes close to the hundreds of thousands to millions of physical qubits required to run Shor's algorithm (the exact number depends on the error rate and correction scheme), let alone practical-level attacks on RSA-2048 or secp256k1. The bottleneck is not only the number of qubits, but also operational fidelity, qubit connectivity, and the sustainable depth of error correction circuitry required to run deep quantum algorithms. While some systems have over 1000 physical qubits, the sheer number is misleading: these systems lack the connectivity and gate fidelity necessary for cryptographically meaningful computation. The physical error rate of recent systems is approaching the range where "quantum error correction begins to work effectively," but no one has yet demonstrated more than a small number of logical qubits with sustainable error correction circuit depth... let alone the thousands of high-fidelity, deep-circuit, fault-tolerant logical qubits required to truly run Shor's algorithm. There is still a huge gap between "proving the principle of quantum error correction is feasible" and "achieving a scale usable for cryptanalysis." In short: CRQC remains a long way off until both the number of qubits and fidelity increase by several orders of magnitude. However, corporate press releases and media reports can be confusing. Common misconceptions include: Demonstrations claiming to achieve "quantum advantage" are often aimed at artificially designed tasks. These tasks are not valuable because of their practical applications, but because they can run on existing hardware and appear to demonstrate a significant quantum speedup—a fact often deliberately downplayed in the announcements. Some companies claim to have thousands of physical qubits, but this usually refers to a quantum annealing machine, not the gate model quantum computer needed to run Shor's algorithm and attack public-key cryptography. Some companies overuse the term "logical qubits." Physical qubits are noisy, and quantum algorithms require logical qubits; Shor's algorithm requires thousands of logical qubits. Quantum error correction allows the construction of a logical qubit using many physical qubits—typically hundreds to thousands of physical qubits per logical qubit, depending on the error rate. However, some companies misuse the term: for example, one announcement claimed to have achieved 48 logical qubits using a distance-2 encoding, requiring only two physical qubits per logical qubit. This is absurd: a distance-2 encoding can only detect errors, not correct them. Truly fault-tolerant logical qubits for cryptanalysis require hundreds to thousands of physical qubits each, not two. More generally, many roadmaps also refer to qubits that only support Clifford operations as "logical qubits." However, Clifford operations can be efficiently simulated by classical computers, insufficient for running Shor's algorithm; Shor requires a large number of error-corrected T-gates (or more generally, non-Clifford gates). Even if the roadmap states "achieving thousands of logical qubits by a certain year," it doesn't mean that Shor's algorithm will be able to break traditional cryptography in the same year. These practices severely distort the public's (and even many professional observers') perception of how close we are to CRQC. Of course, some experts are indeed excited about the progress. For example, Scott Aaronson recently wrote that, given the "astonishing pace of current hardware advancements," he believes we "might" see a fault-tolerant quantum computer running Shor's algorithm before the next US presidential election. However, Aaronson later clarified that what he said did not mean CRQC: even if a fully fault-tolerant run of Shor's algorithm only yields 15 = 3 × 5 (which is faster with pen and paper), he would still consider it an achievement. The threshold remains a very small-scale execution of Shor's algorithm, not the kind with cryptographic relevance; previous experiments on quantum computers that "factored 15" mostly used simplified circuits rather than fully fault-tolerant Shor's algorithms. And there's a reason why these experiments always choose 15: modulo 15 arithmetic is easy, while even slightly larger numbers like 21 are much more difficult. Therefore, quantum experiments claiming to factor 21 often rely on extra hints or shortcuts. Ultimately, the expectation that a CRQC capable of practically breaking RSA-2048 or secp256k1 (which is what real-world cryptography is truly concerned with) will emerge within the next 5 years lacks supporting evidence of publicly available progress. Even a 10-year timeframe is still aggressive. Considering how far we are from CRQC, excitement about progress is perfectly valid alongside a "timeline of more than ten years." So why has the US government set 2035 as the target node for quantum migration after the full completion of its government systems? I believe this is a reasonable completion timeline for such a large-scale migration. However, this does not presume that CRQC will necessarily emerge by then. Where (and where) does an HNDL attack apply (and not)? "Collect-then-decrypt" (HNDL) means that attackers are storing encrypted communications on a large scale now, intending to decrypt them later when CRQC (Confidentiality, Quality, and Capture) is available. Nation-state adversaries may already be archiving encrypted communications from targets like the US government on a large scale, so they can decrypt them years later when CRQC is available. This is why encryption needs to be switched to sooner rather than later—at least for those with confidentiality needs for 10–50 years or more. However, digital signatures (which almost all blockchains rely on) are different from encryption: signatures do not involve "confidentiality," and there is no secret that can be "decrypted afterward." In other words, if CRQC arrives, it will indeed be possible to forge signatures from that moment on; however, past signatures are not "secret" like encrypted messages. As long as you know a signature was generated before CRQC, it cannot be forged. Therefore, the urgency of migrating to post-quantum digital signatures is lower than that of migrating to encryption. Large platforms are also acting according to this logic: Chrome and Cloudflare have deployed a hybrid scheme of X25519 + ML-KEM in TLS for transport layer encryption (for ease of explanation, I use "encryption scheme"; strictly speaking, TLS uses a key exchange or key encapsulation mechanism, not public-key encryption itself). The term "hybrid" here refers to combining a post-quantum secure scheme (ML-KEM) with an existing scheme (X25519) to achieve a combined security guarantee: ML-KEM is intended to defend against HNDL attacks while retaining the classical security of X25519 in case ML-KEM proves insecure even for today's computers. Apple's iMessage also deploys a similar hybrid post-quantum encryption using the PQ3 protocol, while Signal has adopted the same approach in the PQXDH and SPQR protocols. In contrast, the rollout of post-quantum digital signatures on critical web infrastructure has been deliberately delayed until CRQC is truly imminent, as existing post-quantum signature schemes would introduce significant performance regressions (discussed later). zkSNARKs (zero-knowledge concise non-interactive proofs of knowledge) – crucial for the long-term scalability and privacy of blockchains – are similar in risk profile to signatures. This is because even if some zkSNARKs are not post-quantum secure (they use elliptic curve cryptography, just like non-post-quantum encryption and signatures today), their zero-knowledge properties are still post-quantum secure. The zero-knowledge property guarantees that the proof will not reveal any information about the secret witness – even against a quantum adversary – therefore, there is no confidential information that can be “collected now and decrypted later.” Therefore, zkSNARKs are unaffected by HNDL attacks. Just as non-post-quantum signatures generated today remain secure, a zkSNARK proof is credible (i.e., the proved statement must be true) as long as it was generated before CRQC—even if the zkSNARK relies on elliptic curve cryptography. Only after CRQC can an attacker forge seemingly credible proofs for "false statements." What does this mean for blockchains? Most blockchains are not exposed to HNDL attacks: Most non-privacy chains (such as Bitcoin and Ethereum today) use non-post-quantum cryptography primarily for transaction authorization—that is, using digital signatures rather than encryption. To reiterate: signatures do not pose a HNDL risk; HNDL targets encrypted data. For example, Bitcoin's on-chain data is public, and the quantum threat is forging signatures (deriving private keys to steal coins), not "decrypting publicly available transaction data." Therefore, HNDL does not create a cryptographic urgency of "immediate migration." Unfortunately, even analyses from some credible sources (such as the Federal Reserve) have incorrectly claimed that Bitcoin is vulnerable to HNDL attacks, which exaggerates the urgency of a backward quantum cryptography migration. Of course, reduced urgency does not mean Bitcoin can delay: it faces another kind of time pressure—the enormous social coordination costs required to change the protocol (Bitcoin's unique challenges will be discussed in more detail below). The exception so far is privacy chains: many of these encrypt or hide the recipient and amount of funds. This type of confidentiality can be "collected" now and retroactively deanonymized once quantum computers can break elliptic curve cryptography in the future. For privacy chains, the severity of an attack depends on the chain's design. For example, Monero's curve-based ring signatures and key images (linkable tags for each output to prevent double-spending) make it possible to reconstruct large amounts of spending graphs afterward using only the public ledger. In other designs, the damage is more limited—see the discussion by Zcash cryptographer and researcher Sean Bowe. If users truly value the principle that "their transactions should not be exposed even if CRQC emerges in the future," then privacy chains should migrate to post-quantum primitives (or hybrid schemes) as soon as feasible and performance acceptable, or adopt an architecture that avoids putting decryptable secrets on-chain. Bitcoin's Unique Trouble For Bitcoin, the urgency to begin switching to post-quantum signatures stems primarily from two realities, neither of which are inherently related to quantum technology itself. First is the speed of governance: Bitcoin upgrades are extremely slow. Once a dispute arises, the community may be unable to reach a consensus on a suitable solution, triggering a destructive hard fork. Secondly, Bitcoin migration to quantum signature mode cannot be passively completed; holders must actively migrate. This means that abandoned coins exposed to quantum risks cannot be protected. Some estimates suggest that millions of these "quantum-fragile and potentially abandoned" BTC exist, potentially worth hundreds of billions of dollars in December 2025. However, the threat of quantum mechanics to Bitcoin will not be an overnight apocalypse, but rather a "selective, gradual" attack. Quantum computers cannot crack all keys simultaneously—Shor's algorithm must attack each public key individually. Early quantum attacks will be extremely expensive and slow, so once quantum computing can crack a single Bitcoin signature key, attackers will prioritize high-value wallets. Furthermore, as long as users avoid address reuse and do not use Taproot addresses that expose public keys directly on-chain, they are largely secure even without modifying the protocol: their public keys are hidden behind the hash function before spending. The public keys are only revealed when they broadcast the spending transaction, at which point a brief "real-time race" occurs: honest spenders want the transaction confirmed as quickly as possible, while attackers with quantum capabilities attempt to deduce the private key and spend it before final confirmation. Therefore, the truly vulnerable coins are those whose public keys are already exposed on-chain: early P2PK outputs, reused addresses, and Taproot holdings. For abandoned, vulnerable coins, there is no easy solution. Possible options include: The community sets a "flag day," after which all unmigrated coins are considered destroyed; or these abandoned and quantum-fragile coins remain available for anyone with CRQC to seize. The second option raises serious legal and security concerns: even claiming legal ownership or acting in good faith, using a quantum computer to "seize" coins without a private key could trigger theft and computer fraud laws in many jurisdictions. Furthermore, the "abandonment" itself is based on the assumption of long-term inactivity; no one can be certain whether these coins still have a living owner holding the key. Even if you can prove you once owned these coins, it may not constitute sufficient legal authorization for you to "break the encryption" and retrieve them. This legal uncertainty actually increases the risk: ultimately, it's more likely that malicious actors who disregard legal constraints will take these coins. Bitcoin also has a practical limitation: low throughput. Even if a migration plan is finalized, migrating all quantum-fragile funds to post-quantum-secure addresses would take months at Bitcoin's current transaction rate. These challenges mean that Bitcoin must start planning its post-quantum migration now—not because the probability of a CRQC appearing before 2030 is high, but because the technical and operational arrangements for governing, coordinating, and migrating billions or even hundreds of billions of dollars themselves will take many years. The threat of quantum mechanics to Bitcoin is real, but the time pressure mainly comes from Bitcoin's own constraints, not from the "imminent arrival of quantum computers." Other blockchains also face the challenge of quantum-fragile fund migration, but Bitcoin is particularly vulnerable: its earliest transactions used P2PK outputs that directly put public keys on the chain, making a significant portion of BTC more susceptible to CRQC attacks. This technological difference, coupled with Bitcoin's long history, concentrated value, low throughput, and rigid governance, exacerbates the problem. It's important to note that the above vulnerability refers to the cryptographic security of Bitcoin's digital signatures, but it is not directly equivalent to the economic security of the Bitcoin blockchain. The latter stems from the Proof-of-Work (PoW) consensus mechanism, which is relatively less vulnerable to quantum computing for three reasons: PoW relies on hashing, therefore it is only subject to quadratic speedup from the Grover search algorithm, not the exponential speedup from Shor's algorithm. Grover's search incurs significant engineering overhead in real-world implementations, making it unlikely that quantum computers will achieve even a small real-world speedup in Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work (PoW). Even if a significant speedup were achieved, it would more likely give large quantum miners an advantage over smaller miners, rather than fundamentally undermining Bitcoin's economic security model. The Costs and Risks of Post-Quantum Signatures To understand why blockchains shouldn't rush to deploy post-quantum signatures, it's crucial to understand both the performance costs and the evolving nature of our confidence in post-quantum security. Most post-quantum cryptography is roughly based on five types of methods: Hash, Error Correction Codes, Lattice, Multivariable Quadratic Equation Systems (MQ), and Isogenesis. Why are there so many approaches? Because the security of any post-quantum cryptographic primitive rests on the assumption that quantum computers cannot efficiently solve a particular mathematical problem. The stronger the problem structure, the more efficient the protocol we can construct. However, this is a double-edged sword: the more structures there are, the larger the "attack surface" that attack algorithms can exploit. This creates a fundamental tension: stronger assumptions may lead to better performance, but they also increase the likelihood of flawed assumptions and security vulnerabilities. Overall, hash-based schemes are the most conservative in their security assumptions because we are more confident that quantum computers cannot efficiently attack these protocols; however, their performance is the worst. For example, NIST-standardized hash signatures are 7–8 KB in size even with minimal parameters. In contrast, today's elliptic curve-based digital signatures are only 64 bytes, a difference of about 100 times. Lattice-based schemes are currently the focus of deployment. NIST has selected a single cryptographic scheme for standardization, along with two of the three signature algorithms, all based on lattices. One of these lattice signature schemes, ML-DSA (formerly Dilithium), has a signature size between approximately 2.4KB (128-bit security) and 4.6KB (256-bit security), roughly 40–70 times larger than current elliptic curve signatures. The other lattice scheme, Falcon, has even smaller signatures (666 bytes for Falcon-512 and 1.3KB for Falcon-1024), but it involves complex floating-point operations, which NIST itself has flagged as a particular implementation challenge. Thomas Pornin, one of Falcon's authors, even called it "the most complex cryptographic algorithm I've ever implemented." In terms of security, lattice signatures are much more difficult to implement than elliptic curve signatures: ML-DSA has more sensitive intermediate values ​​and nontrivial rejection sampling logic, requiring additional protection against side-channel and fault-based attacks; Falcon also adds the challenge of constant-time floating-point implementation. In fact, several side-channel attacks targeting Falcon implementations have already been able to recover private keys. These problems pose immediate, real-world risks, not distant CRQC risks. There are good reasons to cautiously deploy higher-performance post-quantum solutions: historically, some leading candidates (such as the MQ-based signature scheme Rainbow and the homologous encryption schemes SIKE/SIDH) were ultimately broken by classical computers—that is, by today's computers, not quantum computers. Moreover, these breaches occurred very late in the NIST standardization process. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the scientific process, but also serves as a reminder that premature standardization and deployment can be counterproductive. This is why internet infrastructure has adopted a more cautious strategy regarding signature migrations. This is particularly noteworthy because once internet cryptographic migrations begin, they often take a very long time. For example, the migration from MD5 and SHA-1—hash functions that have long been technically abandoned by relevant organizations—took many years to be truly implemented at the infrastructure level, and in some scenarios, it is still not fully completed. Furthermore, these algorithms are not just "potentially insecure in the future," but have already been completely compromised. The Unique Challenges of Blockchain Compared to Internet Infrastructure On the one hand, blockchains like Ethereum or Solana, continuously maintained by open-source communities, can upgrade faster than traditional internet infrastructure. On the other hand, traditional internet infrastructure benefits from frequent key rotations, allowing its attack surface to move much faster than early quantum machines attacking keys one by one—a luxury blockchains lack: coins and their corresponding keys may remain unchanged for a long time. However, overall, blockchains should still follow the internet's "prudent approach" to signature migration: neither faces HNDL attacks at the signature level, and the costs and risks of prematurely migrating to immature quantum solutions remain significant and will not disappear due to key persistence. Additionally, blockchains have certain specific requirements that make premature migration more complex and dangerous: for example, blockchains often need to rapidly aggregate large numbers of signatures. BLS signatures are widely used today because they support highly efficient aggregation, but they lack post-quantum security. Researchers are exploring the use of SNARKs to aggregate post-quantum signatures, a promising direction, but still in its early stages. Regarding SNARKs, the community currently considers hash-based constructions the mainstream option for post-quantum. However, I am confident that lattice-based options will emerge in the coming months to years as attractive alternatives: they perform better in many ways, such as significantly shorter proof lengths—similar to the advantage of lattice signatures being shorter than hash signatures. The bigger problem now: achieving security. For many years to come, implementation-level vulnerabilities will pose a greater security risk than CRQC. For SNARKs, the primary issue is bugs. Digital signatures and encryption schemes are inherently prone to implementation vulnerabilities, and SNARKs are far more complex. In fact, a digital signature can be viewed as a very simple zkSNARK: proving "I know the private key corresponding to this public key, and I authorized this message." For post-quantum signatures, current risks also include implementation attacks such as side-channel attacks and fault injection. These attacks have been thoroughly studied and can indeed extract private keys from deployed systems; they are far more imminent than distant quantum computers. The community needs years to discover and fix SNARK vulnerabilities and harden post-quantum signature implementations to resist side-channel and fault injection attacks. Because the post-quantum SNARK and aggregated signature schemes are still unstable, premature migration to a blockchain could lock it into a suboptimal solution; when a better solution emerges, or when implementation vulnerabilities are discovered, a second migration may be necessary. What should we do? 7 suggestions. Based on the above realities, I offer some suggestions to various stakeholders (from developers to policymakers). The general principle is: take the quantum threat seriously, but don't assume that "CRQC will definitely appear before 2030." This assumption is inconsistent with current public progress. However, we can still, and should, do some things now: #1 Deploy hybrid encryption immediately. At least in scenarios where long-term confidentiality is important and cost is acceptable. Many browsers, CDNs, and messaging applications (such as iMessage and Signal) have already deployed hybrid solutions. Hybrid (post-quantum + classical) encryption can resist HNDL and hedge against the risk that post-quantum solutions may also be insecure in the classical world. #2 Use hash-based signatures immediately in scenarios where size is acceptable. For low-frequency, size-insensitive scenarios like software and firmware updates, hybrid hash signatures should be adopted now (hybridization is to hedge against implementation bugs in new schemes, not because of concerns about the security assumptions of hashing). This is more conservative and provides society with a clear "lifeline": if a very low-probability event occurs—CRQC unexpectedly appears ahead of schedule—we will fall into a bootstrapping problem if we do not have a software update mechanism already protected by post-quantum signatures: we will not even be able to securely distribute post-quantum cryptographic updates used to patch the system. #3 Blockchain does not need to rush to deploy post-quantum signatures, but should start planning now. Blockchain developers should follow the approach of the Web PKI community and proceed cautiously with signature migrations, allowing post-quantum signature schemes to continue maturing in terms of performance and security understanding, while also allowing time for system refactoring (adapting to larger signatures, improving aggregation techniques). For Bitcoin and other L1 blockchains: the community needs to define migration paths and develop policies for "abandoned and quantum-fragile funds." Passive migration is not an option, so planning is crucial. Bitcoin's unique challenges are mostly non-technical factors—slow governance and a large number of high-value, potentially abandoned, and vulnerable addresses—therefore the Bitcoin community especially needs to start planning now. Simultaneously, research on post-quantum SNARKs and aggregable signatures should be allowed to continue maturing (likely several more years). Premature migrations risk locking in suboptimal solutions or being forced into secondary migrations due to implementation vulnerabilities. A supplement to the Ethereum account model: Ethereum has two types of accounts with different implications for migration—External Accounts (EOAs, controlled by the secp256k1 private key) and smart contract wallets (programmable authorization logic). In non-emergency situations, if Ethereum adds post-quantum signature support, upgradable smart contract wallets can switch to post-quantum verification through contract upgrades; while EOAs may require migrating funds to a new post-quantum secure address (Ethereum may also provide a dedicated migration mechanism for EOAs). In quantum emergency situations, Ethereum researchers have proposed a hard fork solution: freezing vulnerable accounts and allowing users to recover funds by proving they know the mnemonic phrase through a post-quantum secure SNARK. This mechanism applies to both EOAs and unupgraded contract wallets. The practical implications for users are: well-audited, upgradable smart contract wallets may offer a slightly smoother migration path, but the advantages are limited and involve trade-offs in trust with the wallet provider and upgrade governance. More important than account types: The Ethereum community continues to advance research into post-quantum primitives and contingency plans. A broader implication for builders is that many chains strongly bind account identity to specific cryptographic primitives—Bitcoin binds to ECDSA/secp256k1 for Ethereum, and to EdDSA for other chains. Post-quantum migration highlights the value of decoupling account identity from specific signature schemes. Ethereum's move towards smart accounts, and the account abstraction efforts of other chains, embody this trend: allowing accounts to upgrade their authentication logic without abandoning their on-chain history and state. Decoupling doesn't make migration easier, but it is certainly more flexible than hardcoding accounts to a single signature scheme (and also supports other features such as fee payment, social recovery, multisignature, etc.). #4 For privacy chains: Migration should be prioritized and implemented earlier, provided performance is acceptable. The confidentiality of users on these chains is currently exposed to HNDL attacks, although the severity varies depending on the design. The risk is most pressing for designs where complete deanonymization can be achieved solely through a public ledger. A hybrid approach (post-quantum + classical) could be considered to hedge against the risk that "nominally post-quantum" solutions may ultimately be insecure in the classical world, or architectural adjustments could be made to avoid placing decryptable secrets on the chain. #5 Near-end priorities should focus on achieving security, rather than mitigating quantum threats. Especially for complex primitives like SNARKs and post-quantum signatures, bugs and implementation attacks (side-channel attacks, fault injection) are far more likely to be the primary risks than CRQC in the coming years. Invest now in auditing, fuzzing, formal verification, and defense-in-depth and layered security—don't let quantum anxiety overshadow the more pressing threat of bugs. #6 Funding Quantum Computing Research and Development. A key national security implication of all of the above is the need for sustained investment in funding and talent to develop quantum computing. If a major adversary acquires CRQC capabilities before the United States, it will pose a serious national security risk to the United States and the world. #7 Maintain a sober perspective on quantum computing announcements. As hardware matures over the next few years, milestones will continue to emerge. In a sense, the more frequent the announcements, the further we are from CRQC: each milestone is just one of many bridges to cross, and each one will trigger a new round of sensational headlines and excitement. Treat press releases as progress reports requiring critical evaluation, not as signals to trigger hasty action. Of course, the timeline may accelerate due to unexpected innovations or lengthen due to severe scaling bottlenecks. I wouldn't say that "CRQC will appear within five years" is logically absolutely impossible, just extremely unlikely. The above recommendations are robust to this uncertainty: following them can avoid closer, higher-probability risks—implementation bugs, rushed deployments, and those common failures in password migrations.</div></div>

data.title: Prediction Market Kalshi Opened A DC Office To Lobby Regulators Directly
data.description:
Kalshi Expands Government Relations With Office In D.C

Prediction market heavyweight Kalshi is pushing deeper into the heart of U.S. politics, announcing plans to open a Washington, D.C. office and recruit seasoned policy insiders from both sides of the aisle. The move signals a deliberate escalation in Kalshi’s engagement with lawmakers as prediction markets face mounting scrutiny ahead of the 2026 election cycle.

Kalshi confirmed the hiring of John Bivona, a former Biden administration official, as its head of federal government relations, alongside Blake Bee, a former Amazon senior manager who led state and local public policy efforts. Together, the hires give Kalshi direct experience navigating both federal agencies and state regulators at a time when its core business model is under the microscope.

Kalshi prepares for change in control of Congress; hires Democrat to lead DC lobbying efforts. pic.twitter.com/1qpce9v6JZ

— Mick Bransfield (@MickBransfield) January 27, 2026

In a statement, Kalshi's spokesperson emphasized that the company “acts as a neutral platform” and is actively engaging with policymakers and stakeholders to shape how event-based markets are understood and regulated in the U.S. The company has also indicated it is preparing for deeper involvement in policy discussions tied to the 2026 elections.

The expansion comes as prediction markets, including Kalshi and rival platform Polymarket, face legal challenges in at least four U.S. states over allegations of offering unlicensed sports betting. While a 2024 court ruling cleared the way for election-related event contracts, the broader regulatory environment remains fragmented and contentious.

Dominance in a Rapidly Growing Prediction Market

Kalshi enters Washington from a position of strength. According to Dune Analytics data from Jan. 12, the platform accounts for roughly two-thirds of all prediction market trading volume, cementing its status as the sector’s dominant player. The surge in activity followed legal clarity around election betting, drawing users seeking real-time market-based probabilities on political outcomes.

Top Prediction Markets by Weekly Notional Volume

Opinion led in volume last week, with a 3% increase

1. @opinionlabsxyz - $1.95B
2. @Polymarket - $1.75B
3. @Kalshi - $1.7B
4. @predictdotfun - $143M
5. @trylimitless - $56M

Total Weekly Prediction Market Volume reached $5.6B… pic.twitter.com/LUB9ZkkRLw

— ODDScreeners (@ODDScreeners) January 26, 2026

That dominance, however, has also made Kalshi a focal point for regulators and lawmakers concerned about the societal and political implications of betting on elections, public policy outcomes, and government stability. As prediction markets grow in scale and influence, questions around oversight, market manipulation, and public trust are becoming harder for policymakers to ignore.

The regulatory push coincides with heightened market activity tied to looming political events. With U.S. lawmakers racing to pass a funding bill before a Saturday midnight deadline, Kalshi users were pricing in a 78% chance of a government shutdown at the time of publication. Polymarket showed similar odds, reflecting growing pessimism over bipartisan negotiations.

From US raids to Portuguese bans, Polymarket is under scrutiny again for political betting. Will regulators worldwide tighten the rules? ➡️ https://t.co/V8NMZX8IvGpic.twitter.com/BpcCprQ6Wc

— Casino Beats (@CasinoBeatsCom) January 26, 2026

Democrats have been pressing for changes to Department of Homeland Security funding, particularly around Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol operations in major U.S. cities. The standoff has revived memories of October’s 43-day shutdown — the longest in U.S. history — underscoring how prediction markets are increasingly tracking, and potentially amplifying, political risk in real time.

Kalshi’s expansion into Washington suggests the company is no longer content to simply list political outcomes — it wants a seat at the table where the rules governing those markets are written. Whether that strategy secures long-term legitimacy or invites tighter scrutiny may define the future of prediction markets in the U.S.

data.title: The sixth episode of the third season of Blockchain Big Players, titled "Survivors," is officially scheduled to premiere on February 3rd.
data.description:
The sixth episode of the third season of "Blockchain Big Players," the first light variety show in the Web3 industry, jointly launched by Jinse Finance, Twinkle, and Dashu Finance, and produced by HTX DAO and OracleX, is officially scheduled to premiere on February 3, 2026.

Sixth Episode: Introduction to "Survivors"

If you have a natural "organizational buff," the sixth episode of "Survivors" is your stage! Five KOLs will form exclusive teams as captains, and 100 ordinary people will choose to join their factions to start intense team competition PKs. The winning team will share a cash prize.
